name: 05-state-transitions
description: Multi-segment session with deliberate state transitions: topic_intro -> claim_made -> decision_point -> lull. Tests that the routing table produces correct mixes across state changes. THE PROMPT-ENGINEERING THESIS DEMONSTRATION.

transcript.chunk: Today's topic is the new pricing structure for the enterprise tier.

classifier.state_evidence: Single chunk introducing 'enterprise tier pricing structure' as the topic. No claims, no questions in flight.

cards.preview: Enterprise pricing usually has three independent levers: base fee, per-seat, and minimum commit.
cards.full_context: Most B2B SaaS enterprise tiers separate flat platform fee, variable per-user usage, and a contractual floor. Each lever signals a different sales motion.
grounded_in: [00:00] Today's topic is the new pricing structure for the enterprise tier.

tuple.core_claim: three levers base per-seat minimum commit

cards.preview: What's the customer pain that triggered redesigning the enterprise pricing now?
cards.full_context: Pricing redesigns usually have a triggering event. Naming the trigger constrains the design space and reveals the success criterion.
grounded_in: [00:00] Today's topic is the new pricing structure for the enterprise tier.

tuple.core_claim: what specific pain motivated this work

cards.preview: Needs caveat: 'enterprise tier' means different things at $5M ARR vs $50M ARR companies.
cards.full_context: At $5M ARR, the enterprise tier is often anything above self-serve. At $50M-plus ARR, it's a separate sales motion with custom contracts.
grounded_in: [00:00] Today's topic is the new pricing structure for the enterprise tier.

tuple.core_claim: means different things at different company scales

transcript.chunk: Today's topic is the new pricing structure for the enterprise tier.
We're proposing $50k base plus $10 per seat with a 100-seat minimum.

classifier.state_evidence: Speaker proposing concrete pricing: $50k base, $10 per seat, 100-seat minimum.

cards.preview: Verified: $50k base plus 100-seat minimum at $10 per seat implies a $51k floor on annual contract value.
cards.full_context: $50,000 base plus 100 seats at $10/seat = $51,000 minimum annually. The minimum-commit floor is what makes the deal sellable to enterprise procurement.
grounded_in: [00:30] We're proposing $50k base plus $10 per seat with a 100-seat minimum.

tuple.core_claim: $51k minimum annual at the proposed parameters

cards.preview: $10 per seat is unusually low for enterprise — comparables typically land at $40-80/seat at this contract size.
cards.full_context: At $50k base plus 100-seat minimum, a $10/seat marginal rate suggests value capture happens almost entirely in the base. Makes upsell harder once seats grow because per-seat margin is thin.
grounded_in: [00:30] We're proposing $50k base plus $10 per seat with a 100-seat minimum.

tuple.core_claim: $10 per seat is below enterprise comparables

cards.preview: Does the 100-seat minimum apply to seat additions mid-contract, or only at signing and renewal?
cards.full_context: The minimum can be a starting floor (one-time gate at signing) or a sustained floor (must always have 100-plus seats). Different sales and customer-success implications.
grounded_in: [00:30] We're proposing $50k base plus $10 per seat with a 100-seat minimum.

tuple.core_claim: applies at signing or perpetually throughout contract

transcript.chunk: Today's topic is the new pricing structure for the enterprise tier.
We're proposing $50k base plus $10 per seat with a 100-seat minimum.
Should we sell it bundled or as separate SKUs? Marketing wants bundled, sales wants separate.

classifier.state_evidence: Explicit decision being weighed (bundled vs separate SKUs) with two named stakeholder groups disagreeing (marketing vs sales).

cards.preview: What does the customer typically buy first — the bundle or one specific SKU? That determines packaging.
cards.full_context: If customers consistently land on a single high-value SKU and add others later, separate SKUs make the entry purchase easier. If they buy the platform story upfront, bundled wins.
grounded_in: [01:00] Should we sell it bundled or as separate SKUs?

tuple.core_claim: first-buy SKU pattern determines packaging choice

cards.preview: Marketing-vs-sales bundle disagreements usually trace to different metrics — top-of-funnel vs deal velocity.
cards.full_context: Marketing tends to favor bundled because it simplifies positioning. Sales tends to favor separate because it creates flexible entry points. The disagreement isn't really about packaging.
grounded_in: [01:00] Marketing wants bundled, sales wants separate.

tuple.core_claim: packaging disagreement rooted in different team metrics

cards.preview: Ship separate SKUs with an optional bundle discount. Sales gets per-SKU flexibility; marketing leads with the bundle.
cards.full_context: Separate-SKUs-with-bundle-discount is a common compromise: sales gets per-SKU flexibility, marketing gets a bundled hero product, customers can self-select.
grounded_in: [01:00] Should we sell it bundled or as separate SKUs? Marketing wants bundled, sales wants separate.

tuple.core_claim: ship separate SKUs with optional bundle discount

transcript.chunk: Today's topic is the new pricing structure for the enterprise tier.
We're proposing $50k base plus $10 per seat with a 100-seat minimum.
Should we sell it bundled or as separate SKUs? Marketing wants bundled, sales wants separate.

classifier.state_evidence: Latest chunk is pure thinking-out-loud filler ('Hmm. Yeah. Let me think.') with no new content. Conversation has stalled on the decision.

cards.preview: Does the bundled-vs-separate decision need to land today, or is this a 'come back next meeting' question?
cards.full_context: Forcing-function questions help speakers decide whether to push or park. Knowing the urgency context unlocks the next move.

tuple.core_claim: is decision needed today or deferrable

cards.preview: What part of the packaging question is hardest — customer signal, internal alignment, or pricing impact?
cards.full_context: Decomposing the stuck point surfaces what data or alignment is missing. Often the lull happens because three different uncertainties got tangled together.

tuple.core_claim: what specifically is hardest to resolve

cards.preview: Is there someone outside this conversation whose input would unstick this — a customer, exec, or finance partner?
cards.full_context: Lulls often signal a missing voice in the room. Naming who that voice would be is faster than continuing to think in a circle.

tuple.core_claim: what external input would unstick this
